original_hypothesis.title: Token Issuer Can Arbitrarily Confiscate User Vesting Allocations
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The `transferVesting` function in `SecondSwap_StepVesting.sol` allows the `tokenIssuer` to transfer vesting allocations from any `_grantor` to any `_beneficiary` without the grantor's consent. A malicious or compromised token issuer can arbitrarily confiscate vested tokens from users who purchased them on the marketplace.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: While token issuers are semi-trusted, giving them the power to arbitrarily seize user assets breaks the fundamental trust model of a secondary marketplace. Buyers have no guarantee their purchased allocations won't be stolen.
preconditions: Token issuer acts maliciously or their private key is compromised

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Privileged access control allows arbitrary state modification
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Token issuer steals user funds and dumps them on the market.

economic_questions: Is this power explicitly documented and accepted by users?
validation_requirements: Verify that the tokenIssuer can successfully call transferVesting on a user's allocation without their approval.

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Total value of all vested tokens

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When total vested value is high
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete loss of user funds and protocol reputation

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Total Value Locked in the protocol

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
